nativeRange.habitat: The Desert Goby is endemic to artesian spring-fed pools, bores, and intermittent streams in arid central Australia, within the Lake Eyre drainage basin. It is typically found in shallow, often turbid waters over sandy or rocky substrate, and is capable of tolerating extreme fluctuations in temperature (10–35 °C), salinity (from effectively fresh to hypersaline), pH, and dissolved oxygen.
nativeRange.biotope: Arid Australian artesian spring / desert playa

decorPreferences: smooth rocks and flat stones forming caves and crevices
terracotta pots, pipes, and shells as spawning caves
hardy plants such as Vallisneria tolerant of hard, alkaline water (optional)
low to moderate flow with strong filtration
tight-fitting lid — gobies will perch and may jump

diet.notes: The Desert Goby is an opportunistic micropredator and incidental algae-grazer that readily accepts a wide range of prepared foods, with sinking pellets often being a clear favourite alongside quality flake and freeze-dried options. Frozen and live invertebrates such as bloodworm, daphnia, baby brine shrimp, and chopped mysis are also taken eagerly. Small amounts of vegetable matter — blanched spinach or spirulina flake — help round out the diet. Two small feedings per day are appropriate; the species tends to become obese in well-fed home aquaria, so portion control is worthwhile.

goodWith: single male with one or more females
other small, robust hard-water species (e.g., White Cloud Mountain minnows, rainbowfish in larger tanks)
avoidWith: very small fish or fry that fit in the goby's mouth
multiple territorial males in tanks under ~90 L
boisterous, fast feeders that outcompete bottom-dwellers

imageCandidates.notes: Lehtonen T, Svensson P, Wong B; 2671x1910. Male in full nuptial display beside a female, showing the diagnostic black fins with yellow-and-blue dorsal markings; sharp on the male's head, two individuals illustrating courtship.
Annalog~commonswiki; 650x488. Clean side profile of a whole specimen on sand showing the stocky goby body shape and dorsal fin; lower resolution and more muted colour, kept as a supporting view.

summary: The Desert Goby (Chlamydogobius eremius) is a small Australian species reaching 5 to 6 cm, native to artesian springs in the arid Lake Eyre basin. It is widely considered one of the most tolerant freshwater fish in the hobby, enduring extreme swings in temperature, salinity, and pH. Males tend to spar over caves and are best kept with one or more females.
careNotes:
The Desert Goby is widely regarded as one of the most physiologically tolerant freshwater fish in the hobby: wild populations endure water temperatures from roughly 10 °C up to 35 °C, salinities from fresh to far above seawater, and pH values reported as high as 11 in some spring outflows. In the aquarium, mid-range conditions (22–28 °C, pH 7.0–8.5, moderately hard water) suit them well. The species does not require brackish conditions but tolerates them, and a teaspoon of marine salt per approximately 20 L is sometimes added by keepers aiming to replicate the wild environment.

A tank of around 75 L (approximately 20 US gallons) with a footprint of at least 60 cm gives a single male room to defend a cave while females and subordinate fish stake out their own territory. Hobbyist accounts differ on minimum tank size, with some citing as little as 40 L (10 US gallons); a larger setup is generally preferred because males are strongly territorial around spawning caves and undersized tanks can rapidly become hostile. Provide a fine sand substrate — Desert Gobies tend to hop along the bottom rather than swim continuously — along with abundant smooth rocks and caves. A tight-fitting lid is important, as gobies are surprisingly capable jumpers and routinely perch on hardscape near the surface.

Captive lifespan estimates vary between sources; some accounts report as little as one year, while others suggest up to six. A realistic expectation is generally 2–5 years with stable conditions, a varied diet, and the avoidance of prolonged temperature extremes.

Keep one male with one or more females; a second adult male should only be added in larger tanks (≥120 L) with multiple distinct cave territories. The species has been reported as suitable for community tanks with similarly hard-water-tolerant tankmates, but very small fish risk being swallowed and shrimp may be predated.
breedingNotes: Readily bred in the home aquarium and a popular subject for beginner egg-layer breeding projects. The male selects and defends a cave (typically the underside of a flat rock, terracotta pot, or short pipe), develops intense blue-and-yellow nuptial colouration, and performs a fin-flaring courtship display to attract a gravid female. The female deposits roughly 100–300 elongate eggs on the cave ceiling, after which the male evicts her and tends the clutch alone, fanning and guarding for 10–17 days at 27–30 °C. Fry hatch as fully benthic miniatures (no pelagic phase), and accept baby brine shrimp and crushed flake almost immediately. Removing fry to a separate rearing tank improves survival, as both adults and other tankmates will predate them.

primary.name: Seriously Fish — Chlamydogobius eremius
primary.notes: Primary synthesis source after Aquarium Co-Op returned no dedicated species page. Provided size 5.5–6 cm, temperature 20–28 °C, pH 7.0–8.5, hardness 90–357 ppm (~5–20 dH), tank base 45×30 cm, brackish tolerance (1–37.5 ppt in nature), cave-spawning behaviour, paternal care 10–17 days, and the Lake Eyre endemism. Note: Aquarium Co-Op (intended primary) does not host a species page for this fish.

additional.name: FishBase — Chlamydogobius eremius
additional.notes: Confirmed family (Gobiidae), order (Gobiiformes), max total length 6.0 cm, distribution (South Australia, Lake Eyre basin), climate (temperate, 10–35 °C tolerance), facultative air-breathing, salinity tolerance to 0–60 ppt in lab studies, and pH tolerance up to 11.0. Recommended aquarium parameters pH 7.0–8.0, dH 9–19.

additional.name: Aqua Imports — Australian Desert Goby
additional.notes: Trade-source verification: 2.5" adult size (~6.3 cm), 72–80 °F (22.2–26.7 °C), pH 6.8–8.0, 0–10 ppt salinity tolerance, 20 gal minimum tank, peaceful with caveat that males spar and may swallow very small fish. US tank-raised stock.

additional.name: HoustonFishBox — Desert Goby Profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist profile used to cross-check tank size (10 US gal cited — low end of range), hardness 9–19 dH, breeding details (cave ceiling deposition, paternal guarding ~15–17 days), and diet preference for sinking pellets. Lifespan figure given there (~1 year) appears low relative to other sources; final lifespan range averaged with other hobbyist sources (2–5 years).

fish.breedingNotes: The Desert Goby is a cave-spawner in which the female deposits roughly 100–300 eggs on the ceiling of a chosen cave, after which the male tends the clutch alone for 10–17 days at 27–30 °C. Fry are benthic at hatching and accept baby brine shrimp and crushed flake almost immediately, making this one of the more approachable egg-layer breeding projects for beginning hobbyists.
